Among the participants who were sufficiently passionate (N = 1,093; Study 5), the mini meta-analysis of our five samples indicated that excellencism and perfectionistic standards were positively associated with harmonious and obsessive passion, respectively. This study highlighted the need to separate excellencism from perfectionism to better align empirical research with theoretical expectations about the obsessive and inflexible nature of those who pursue perfectionistic standards.
Keywords: Perfection, Excellence, Passion, Persistence, Inflexibility, Obsessivity

articles.title: Teaching Adults to Respond to Suspected Opioid Overdose:  A Proof-of-Concept Evaluation of Behavioral Skills Training
articles.authors: Jason Vladescu; Sarah G. Segal; Lauren K. Schnell-Peskin; Jessica Day-Watkins
articles.abstract: The opioid crisis remains a major public health emergency in the United States, with over 100,000 drug overdose deaths reported in 2023, nearly 80,000 involving opioids. Naloxone can reverse the effects of opioid overdose, but community members may be unprepared to respond effectively. The purpose of the present study was to conduct a proof-of-concept evaluation of behavioral skills training to teach a convenience sample of adults to respond to simulated overdose and non-overdose scenarios. All three participants demonstrated mastery-level responding across the three training scenarios following BST, correct responding during context-generalization probes, and returned high social validity ratings. We discuss these findings in light of previous BST research, directions for future research, and limitations.

articles.title: Morphological analysis when morphemes are hard to detect: evidence from infixation and reduplication
articles.authors: Dave Kenneth Tayao Cayado; Kathleen Rastle
articles.abstract: There is substantial psycholinguistic evidence that skilled reading involves morphological analysis, whereby morphologically complex words like teacher are recognised via constituent morphemes {teach}+{-er}. The ability to recognise morphemes is vital as it allows readers to interpret both familiar and unfamiliar words. However, over 50% of the evidence for this claim comes from studies of suffixation in English, where morphemes typically have a relatively consistent surface form across exemplars (clean in cleaner, cleanliness; -ed in walked, jumped). It remains unclear whether morphological analysis is also robust when morphemes lack a consistent surface form. Using the morpheme interference paradigm in Tagalog, we test whether infixes and reduplicants are detected during prelexical processing. Infixes are inserted within a stem and therefore reduce the consistency of its surface realisation (e.g., tawag → t-in-awag ‘called’), while reduplicants involve partial or full copying of a stem and therefore lack a consistent surface realisation (e.g., tawag and lundag become tatawag ‘will call’ and lulundag ‘will jump’, where ta- and lu- are instances of the same reduplicant morpheme). We found robust morpheme interference effects in both accuracy and RTs. Specifically, nonwords with morphological structure induced significantly lower accuracy and longer RTs than nonwords without. Our findings show skilled readers’ acquired sensitivity to these sophisticated morphological structures and motivate refinements to theories of morpheme learning.

articles.title: Single-trial principal components analysis of event-related potentials: Back-projection, theoretical rationale, empirical evaluation, and reliability
articles.authors: Jürgen Kayser
articles.abstract: Temporal principal components analysis (tPCA) is widely used to identify and quantify event-related potentials (ERPs). It is less commonly applied to single-trial EEG epochs, despite concerns about the low signal-to-noise ratio and uncertainty about how single-trial components compare with those derived from averaged waveforms. Alternatively, averaged principal components (PCs) can be imposed on single trials via back-projection. This study evaluated the fidelity of single-trial and back-projected single-trial tPCA (covariance-based, unrestricted Varimax rotation) in representing interpretable averaged PCs by systematically comparing factor loadings and factor scores in two existing ERP datasets (72-channel, N=152; 67-channel, N=98), using both surface-referenced (infinity, nose) and reference-free current source density (CSD) transformations. In the 72-channel dataset with four balanced conditions (emotional hemifield paradigm), single-trial PCs closely matched those from averaged data, whereas in the 67-channel dataset with three imbalanced conditions (novelty oddball task), they did not. In contrast, across datasets and transformations, back-projected single-trial PCs consistently reproduced virtually identical averaged PCs. All single-trial tPCA solutions enabled full reconstruction of the original data matrix, confirming the methodological validity of back-projected decomposition, and critically, back-projection enabled estimation of internal consistency for PCA-derived component measures at the single-trial level, with all aggregated component scores showing high or adequate dependability. These findings suggest that single-trial tPCA may approximate averaged solutions only when the variance structure is preserved, whereas back-projection provides a robust alternative by quantifying single-trial epochs relative to the averaged covariance structure, thereby maintaining component structure, integrity, and interpretability of trial-level component estimates in continuity with established ERP constructs.

articles.title: LLMorphism: When humans come to see themselves as language models
articles.authors: Valerio Capraro
articles.abstract: LLMorphism is the biased belief that human cognition works like a large language model. I argue that the rise of conversational LLMs may make this bias increasingly psychologically available. When artificial systems produce human-like language, people may draw a reverse inference: if LLMs can speak like humans, perhaps humans think like LLMs. This inference is biased because similarity at the level of linguistic output does not imply similarity in cognitive architecture. Yet, LLMorphism may spread through two mechanisms: analogical transfer, whereby features of LLMs are projected onto humans, and metaphorical availability, whereby LLM vocabulary becomes a culturally salient vocabulary for describing thought. I distinguish LLMorphism from mechanomorphism, anthropomorphism, computationalism, dehumanization, objectification, and predictive-processing theories of mind. I outline its implications for work, education, responsibility, healthcare, communication, creativity, and human dignity, while also discussing boundary conditions and forms of resistance. I conclude that the public debate may be missing half of the problem: the issue is not only whether we are attributing too much mind to machines, but also whether we are beginning to attribute too little mind to humans.

articles.title: Expérience Intérieure : Concepts, Modèles, Méthodes et Implications en Psychologie, une Scoping Review
articles.authors: Coline Grégoire; Sébastien Fablet; Jeremy Villatte
articles.abstract: L’expérience intérieure correspond à un ensemble de phénomène mentaux dont les modalités les plus connues sont le discours intérieur (la « voix dans la tête ») et la vision intérieur (« visualiser des choses dans sa tête). Bien que réputée difficile à étudier expérimentalement, elle fait l’objet d’un intérêt grandissant ces dernières années. L’objectif de cette scoping review est de proposer un état des lieux des travaux sur l’expérience intérieure qui permettra au lecteur de se faire une   vue d’ensemble de ce champ de recherche. Nous commencerons par présenter les principales modalités d’expérience intérieures abordées dans la littérature. Nous présenterons les différents modèles théoriques. Dans une perspective clinique, nous présentons les différents outils développés pour tenter de mesurer l’expérience intérieure. Nous questionnons l’influence des cas particuliers d’anendophasie (absence de discours intérieur) et d’aphantasie (absence de vision intérieure) sur l’évaluation du fonctionnement cognitif et discutons de l’intérêt du discours intérieur dans le cadre de la prise en charge des troubles du langage comme l’aphasie. Pour conclure, l’expérience intérieure est abordée comme objet d’étude interdisciplinaire reliant l’analyse des mécanismes cognitifs et des applications pratiques en psychologie clinique, éducative et de la santé.

articles.title: Stimulus-category confounds reduce, but do not explain, different roles of intent across moral domains: Valid statistical inference helps explain ‘inconsistent’ findings
articles.authors: Joseph Sweetman; Ryan M. McManus
articles.abstract: The reduced role of intent when judging impure, vs. harmful, acts provides evidence that our moral mind/brain may be composed of distinct, domain-specific systems. However, a study that manipulated and statistically adjusted for the target (i.e., self vs. other) of the act reported a significant, but notably smaller, intent × domain effect (Chakroff et al., 2013). Recent studies that either manipulated or controlled for the context, severity, weirdness, and target of the act reported nonsignificant effects (Kupfer et al., 2020; Parkinson & Byrne, 2017). We tested two competing hypotheses to explain these inconsistent findings: the intent × domain effect is a methodological artifact of the stimuli employed, or the effect is merely reduced in magnitude when controlling for potential stimulus-category confounds. To test these hypotheses, we conducted a close replication of Kupfer et al. We found a small, significant intent × domain effect, supporting the reduced-magnitude hypothesis. We find that the “inconsistent” findings can be explained by the reduced magnitude of the effect when employing carefully controlled stimuli, and by whether researchers test support for the null hypothesis, conduct sample size simulations, and perform model checking and appropriate robustness checks when modeling ordinal (i.e., Likert) items as interval data. Indeed, Kupfer et al.’s original null result becomes significant when an ordinal mixed model is fitted. Our findings are consistent with perspectives that view the moral mind/brain as constituted by multiple moral-cognitive modules. However, they are also consistent with a single moral- cognitive system with domain-dependent modulation. While carefully controlled experimental work and valid statistical inference help the replicability, robustness, and reproducibility of psychological science, delineating and decomposing the moral mind/brain will also require reforms to theory development.

articles.title: Virtue Tradeoffs in Everyday life
articles.authors: Qilin Zhang; Jeremy Jacobson; Benjamin Hardin; Jessie Sun
articles.abstract: Is it usually possible to be virtuous in all ways, or do people experience ubiquitous tradeoffs among moral virtues? Past work has examined a limited set of virtue pairs (e.g., honesty vs. compassion, fairness vs. loyalty) and focused on situations where tradeoffs are likely to occur. Across two Day Reconstruction Method studies featuring representative U.S. samples, we describe the frequency and experience of tradeoffs among 12 virtues in everyday life. In Study 1, 377 participants reported virtue conflicts during 12,385 episodes across one week. In Study 2, 608 participants provided detailed information about one tradeoff episode. Tradeoffs most often involved honesty and courage, and qualitative codings showed that many (but not all) tradeoffs reflected a general tension between benevolence and integrity. Overall, however, prevalence estimates suggested that virtue enactments tend to be largely compatible in everyday life. People felt less happy when they experienced a virtue conflict or tradeoff, but it also mattered how they resolved them. People most often prioritized one virtue over the other (57%). Occasionally (12%), however, people were able to overcome the tradeoff by showing high levels of both virtues, and were most satisfied with their decision when they were able to do so. Finally, there was a general mismatch between people’s proclaimed virtue preferences and which virtues they actually prioritized when they experienced an everyday tradeoff. These findings highlight the importance of studying moral decision making in ecologically valid contexts and have implications for moral education.

articles.title: The Effect of Motivational Interviewing on Physical Impairments, Activity Limitations, and Participation Restrictions in Stroke Survivors: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Hanae Tainaka; Kazuaki Oyake; Satoshi Tanaka
articles.abstract:
Background: Motivational interviewing might have beneficial effects on psychological outcomes in stroke survivors, although evidence supporting its effects on physical functional outcomes, including physical impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions, remains insufficient. Since the publication of the Cochrane review in 2015, research on this topic has continued. Therefore, this scoping review aimed to map the evidence published since 2015 on the use of motivational interviewing to improve physical impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions in stroke survivors. 
Methods: This scoping review protocol followed the format outlined in the Joanna Briggs Institute Methods Manual for scoping reviews. We searched Web of Science, PubMed, and Cochrane Library, along with gray literature sources and reference lists of included studies, in November 2024. Studies published in English between January 2015 and November 2024 that investigated the effects of motivational interviewing interventions on physical impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions in stroke survivors were included. The reporting has been conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews guidelines.
Results: The review included five feasibility studies, two randomized controlled trials, one quasi-experimental study, and one trial protocol. Most studies used motivational interviewing in combination with other interventions, with variability in providers, modes of delivery, and doses across studies. All feasibility studies concluded that motivational interviewing interventions were feasible for stroke rehabilitation. Although all six studies performing within-group comparisons reported improvements in at least one physical impairment, activity limitation, or participation restriction, only one of five studies conducting between-group comparisons demonstrated its effectiveness for improving physical impairments and activity limitations.
Conclusion: The use of motivational interviewing for improving physical impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions in stroke rehabilitation has grown since 2015. However, evidence supporting its effectiveness remains insufficient. Large-scale, rigorous randomized controlled trials are needed to strengthen the quality of evidence in this field.

articles.title: Semantic Cognition for and from Language Models
articles.authors: Kanishka Misra
articles.abstract: The success of neural network language models (LMs) at showing semantically sophisticated behavior raises questions about the extent to which they are compatible with human conceptual knowledge, and if they can perhaps also advance our understanding of semantic cognition. This brief opinion explores this potentially bidirectional connection between LMs and semantic cognition. Specifically, I review a range of methodology used to establish the structure and the function of conceptual knowledge in LMs, primarily inspired by the semantic cognition literature. I then summarize three possible ways that future work could take to make contributions in the opposite direction, by using insights from language models to make conclusions about the language-based origin and mechanisms of human semantic cognition.

articles.title: Affective and Psycholinguistic Matching in German-English Words: Norms for Valence, Arousal, Word Frequency and Length for 40 Word Pairs
articles.authors: Michelle Stankovic; Takeshi Hamamura; Britta Biedermann
articles.abstract: This study presents a tightly matched stimulus set varying in valence (positive/negative) and arousal (high/low), based on selected words from two affective databases: the Affective Norms for English Words (Bradley & Lang, 1999), and its German adaptation, the Affective Norms for German Sentiment Terms (Schmidtke et al., 2014). Forty German-English word pairs were matched pairwise on valence, arousal, word frequency (words per million) and word length (letters), and listwise to ensure equivalence across affective categories. Finally, a subset of the items was newly validated by a sample of English speakers to ensure the validity of the norms in a contemporary context. Correlations show that stimulus set was closely matched across valence (r =.94), arousal (r = .92), frequency (r = .97) and length (r =.83). The stimulus set, including both affective and psycholinguistic data is provided online for future use with bilingual speakers.

articles.title: Since the last beep or in the last 30 minutes: The effect of items’ retrospective time frame on their functioning and usability in an experience sampling study
articles.authors: Milla Pihlajamäki; Ginette Lafit; Olivia J Kirtley; Inez Myin-Germeys; Gudrun Eisele
articles.abstract: Experience sampling method (ESM) studies often use items capturing retrospective time frames (e.g., the last hour). However, the effects of retrospective time frames on responses and response processes remain unclear. We experimentally manipulated retrospective time frame (in the last 30/60/90 minutes; since the last beep) in N=192 students. Outcomes included distributional properties of responses, item user-friendliness (ease of responding, burden, response time), and predictive (relationship between stressful events and negative affect) and convergent (relationship between baseline- and ESM-assessed alcohol use) validity. We found that 30-minute time frames yielded strongest predictive validity, whereas 90-minute time frames produced the slowest responses, greatest overlap with adjacent assessment periods, and lowest convergent validity. In the since the last beep condition, time frame length predicted longer response times. We recommend matching time frames to study goals, and transparently reporting on the choice, wording, and analysis of items with retrospective time frames.

articles.title: Pupillary Indices of Cognitive Workload in (In-)Efficient Reference Production
articles.authors: Elli Tourtouri; Nicole Gotzner
articles.abstract: Speakers’ use of redundant information in referential communication has been the subject of much debate, as it seemingly defies Grice’s Maxim of Quantity (Grice, 1975). While some research argues for an egocentric view on referential redundancy (i.e., that it results from a concern to ease production effort), other work supports an audience-design view (i.e., that speakers strive for communicative efficiency). This research has mainly leveraged listeners’ comprehension as an indirect index of production strategy, while it is yet unclear whether audience-design indeed incurs increased cognitive effort for the speaker. In this study, we used pupillometry to directly assess the cognitive load associated with different referential production strategies, finding evidence for modulated effort based on speakers’ engagement in reasoning about the efficiency of their utterances.

articles.title: Mothers’ Smartphone Use, Phone-Related Distress and Empowerment, and Maternal and Infant Mood and Behavior: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Approach
articles.authors: Brandon T. McDaniel; Sarah Coyne; Lara Wolfers; Adam M Galovan; Rachel Pfafman; Aniruddha Shinde; Alison K Ventura
articles.abstract: Although parents often use phones while around their infants, little research has looked at how this relates to mothers’ mood and their perceptions of their infant and their parenting on a momentary basis. The current study captured maternal phone use and perceptions in real time, namely perceptions of phone-related distress and empowerment, particularly during periods when mothers were with their infant. Participants included 160 mothers of 2-month-old children. Measures included objective passive sensing of smartphone use and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) across 5 days to investigate associations between maternal phone use, maternal mood, and perceptions of infant and parenting behaviors. Overall, objective phone use was often not associated with maternal mood or perceptions of infant or parenting behavior. In contrast, mothers’ subjective experiences of their phone use were related to these outcomes. For example, perceiving technoference or feeling worse about oneself or one’s parenting due to phone use was linked to greater maternal emotional distress and loneliness, as well as perceptions of delayed responsiveness, more infant distress, and lower infant happiness. In contrast, phone-related empowerment showed relatively few associations with maternal or infant outcomes, although empowerment may accumulate over the day to contribute to lower maternal emotional distress. Distinguishing between phone-related distress and empowerment may be especially valuable for guiding future research and future messaging to mothers. Encouraging greater self-awareness and mindfulness around phone use may help mothers engage with their devices in ways that support, rather than undermine, their mental health and parenting.

articles.title: Distributed Atrophy 2.0: Cognitive Offloading and the Reshaping of Thought
articles.authors: Arya Jadhav
articles.abstract: AI tools no longer just assist our thinking—they shape it. This paper introduces the concept of distributed atrophy: the gradual weakening of internal cognitive abilities through habitual reliance on intelligent systems. Drawing from distributed cognition, embodied intelligence, and cognitive offloading theory, we argue that modern AI doesn’t just support mental work—it rewires it. As users adapt to streamlined information flows and instant synthesis, key capacities like abstraction, synthesis, and metacognitive pause may erode. We propose a model of offloading schemas—internalized patterns for delegating thought to machines—and explore how these emerging blueprints may shift how we learn, create, and define intelligence itself.

articles.title: The Temporal Discounting of Moral Judgments
articles.authors: Charlotte Austin; Jordan Wylie
articles.abstract: When in time do people stop judging past actions by today's moral standards? Across two experiments, we tested whether and how time—ranging from today to medieval times—affects moral condemnation across a range of actions. In Experiment 1, we found that as temporal distance increased, participants were less likely to apply modern moral standards. However, highly harmful transgresions (e.g., sexual assault) showed attenuated discounting. Experiment 2 replicated and extended these findings—testing whether perceived knowledge of someone in the past explains why temporal discounting occurs. We find evidence that belief that a transgressor had knowledge of the action being a moral wrong partially explains whether people apply their current moral standards. These results suggest that people stop applying their own moral standards when evaluating the past and do so based on both harm and inferences that a person should have known the action was wrong.

articles.title: Group Identity, Moral Expansiveness, and Generosity Across 69 Countries
articles.authors: Kyle Fiore Law; Zizhong Xiao; Stylianos Syropoulos
articles.abstract: Group identity is theorized to shape how widely people extend moral concern. Yet, expansive moral concern is often assumed to undermine local bonds. Using data from 51,404 adults across 69 countries, we tested whether distinct forms of group connection predict moral circle expansiveness (MCE) and whether MCE predicts generosity in a zero-sum allocation task. In multilevel models, social belongingness predicted higher MCE, whereas collective narcissism predicted lower MCE; national identification was unrelated. MCE, in turn, predicted keeping less for oneself and allocating more to both national and international charities while controlling for demographics. Demographic correlates were also robust. Specifically, older age, higher subjective SES, being female, and left-leaning ideology predicted greater MCE. Country-level follow-ups showed heterogeneity in significance but broadly consistent directional patterns, particularly for behavioral outcomes. These findings suggest expansive moral concern is compatible with secure connection, constrained by exclusionary identity, and meaningfully linked to generosity across diverse societies.

articles.title: Actively seeking variability: effects of active learning and talker variability on novel word learning in adults
articles.authors: Federica Bulgarelli; Marina Rabideau
articles.abstract: Talker variability has been found to be both helpful and challenging for learners. For example, while adult word recognition is slowed down when the talker changes rapidly from trial to trial, this same talker variability can benefit learning non-native sound contrasts and production of novel words. However, all prior research has exposed participants to predetermined orders or variability conditions. Since research suggests that learners engage in active information seeking and it can benefit learning, here we ask whether adults seek out talker variability during word learning and how this variability influences performance. Active participants chose to hear words from 5 talkers on average, and hearing more talker variability for individual words predicted production and comprehension accuracy. Performance did not differ for participants in the Active and Passive conditions. Results are discussed in the context of high variability phonetic training and active learning theories.

articles.title: The Relationship Between Short-Form Video Use and Sustained Attention
articles.authors: Kerria Drüppel; Jana Dombrowski; Sabine Trepte
articles.abstract: Short-form video platforms such as TikTok are accused of negatively impacting users’ ability to sustain attention. In our study, we investigate the relationship between short-form video use and sustained attention. Participants (N = 385) of our study completed a real-time response task and provided self-assessments of attention. According to our results, the s short-form video use is related with attentional deficits. We suggest complementing media literacy with the notion of attention literacy and will discuss the latter as an option for further research in a world with an increasing number of choices to face when it comes to the distribution of attention.

articles.title: Building world models by learning to distinguish imagination from reality: why childhood imagination feels so real
articles.authors: Ataol Burak Ozsu; Nora Petrova; Tessa Dekker; Nadine Dijkstra
articles.abstract: To be able to generate imagination, the brain first needs to acquire an internal model of the world from which to generate. However, as our world model develops as we get older, it seems that our ability to vividly imagine decreases rather than increases. We propose that this paradox arises because world models co-develop with the ability to distinguish imagination from reality. We synthesize insights from generative adversarial networks, a class of machine learning models that learn a generative model of training data via an adversarial feedback loop with a discriminator. The discriminator learns to determine whether data is real or generated, while the generator learns to generate data that is realistic enough to fool the discriminator. Through simulations we show that an early stage of coarse generation combined with poor discrimination is necessary for a stable learning regime. These results suggest that children may have more vivid imagination precisely because their model of reality is less developed. We further find that the discriminator relies on different features over training, suggesting that reality monitoring might utilise different information throughout development. This framework explains how the brain learns to simulate the world while staying grounded in reality.

articles.title: Through Human Eye: Can a Robot Possess a Sense of Self?
articles.authors: Kristina Nikolovska
articles.abstract: In recent years, there has been significant progress in developing service robots for various settings, prompting a need to examine user--robot interactions. This paper investigates how implementing specific self-characteristics in non-humanoid robots influences user perception. We propose a return to fundamental self-aspects to understand their impact on robot perception. The study examines five fundamental attributes of selfhood (causality, equifinality, behavioral efficiency, learning sensitivity, context sensitivity) and their influence on user perception, utilizing a vehicle-like Duckiebot robot in a controlled experimental setting. By simplifying the complexity of selfhood into manageable traits, we aim to streamline robot development for smoother integration into diverse environments. Our ongoing research aims to contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of human--robot interactions and the development of user-friendly robotic systems that foster positive, trustworthy user experiences.

articles.title: The Blind Spot in Research on Nature’s Benefits: Restorative versus Instorative Effects and Potential Biases.
articles.authors: Johanna Prugger; Simone Kühn
articles.abstract: Environmental psychology has extensively documented the restorative effects of nature: the recovery of cognitive resources and emotional well-being following stress or fatigue. Yet, the field may have overlooked instorative effects, where nature enhances functioning from a neutral baseline without prior depletion. We reviewed 54 studies (61 experiments, 19962024) investigating nature versus urban exposure effects on cognition, affect, and perceived restoration. Studies were categorized as explicit stressor (deliberate pre-exposure stress induction), implicit stressor (pre-exposure assessments likely inducing fatigue), or instorative (no prior stressor/assessment). Using an iterative, data-driven approach inspired by Grounded Theory, we analyzed study characteristics, exposure types, and the proportion reporting beneficial effects. Restorative studies vastly outnumbered instorative studies (45 vs. 8), revealing substantial research bias. Despite limited representation, instorative studies demonstrated high success rates: all cognition studies reported positive effects, while two-thirds examining affect showed benefits. Explicit stressor studies showed stronger effects than implicit stressor studies, suggesting dose-response relationships. Differentiating between instorative and restorative effects remains difficult, as no validated tools specifically assess instoration. The widely used Perceived Restorativeness Scale primarily measures general environmental benefits, conflating restoration and instoration. Studies predominantly employed young, Western samples and brief laboratory exposures, limiting ecological validity. The capacity of nature to enhance, rather than merely restore, functioning is underexplored. Future research should investigate instorative effects using ecologically valid paradigms with neutral baselines, refined theoretical models, standardized terminology, and appropriate questionnaires that distinguish between instorative and restorative effects.

articles.title: Changing moral beliefs and behaviors using human-AI dialogues
articles.authors: Joshua Paul White; Carter Allen; Lucius Caviola; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: Moral beliefs are widely considered resistant to change, yet the rise of conversational AI offers new tools for moral persuasion. We report a preregistered experiment (N = 500) in which participants were randomly assigned to a 5-round conversation with an AI agent prompted to persuade them that either eating meat, or not giving to charity, is morally wrong. Conversations produced large and immediate shifts in moral beliefs in the intended direction (Cohen’s d = 0.37–0.48), along with corresponding behavioral effects: participants in the charity conversation condition donated 16.35% more of their bonus money to charity than participants in the meat conversation condition, while those in the meat conversation condition were 25.04 percentage points more likely to choose a plant-based over a beef burger voucher compared to those in the charity conversation condition. One month later, the effect on burger voucher choice remained significant, while other effects did not. NLP analysis of participant moral justifications and AI persuasive strategies suggests that moral persuasion was partly driven by factual correction—particularly in the meat domain, where factual appeals to the naturalness and biological necessity of meat eating were the most common among participants—alongside direct moral argumentation. These results demonstrate that human-AI dialogues can produce meaningful change in moral beliefs and behavior, raising important questions about the capacity of conversational AI to shape human values at scale.

articles.title: Human sources of social bias in automated face classification
articles.authors: Jeffrey Jordan Berg
articles.abstract:
How do human sources of social bias introduce and exacerbate inequalities in automated face
classification? In this dissertation, I discuss existing work on demographic bias in machine
learning and computer vision, before empirically examining two points of entry for human social
bias in face classification models: the choice of training dataset and the annotation of
demographic labels. In Chapter 2, I demonstrate how dataset racial diversity impacts race-
specific classification accuracy and the classification of mixed race faces. In Chapter 3, I show
how annotator racial prejudice has significant downstream effects on face classification model
performance and model classification of racial ambiguity. Lastly, I discuss the importance of
dataset construction, the challenges of race labeling, and how humans perceive algorithmic
output, as crucial issues in the spread of prejudice through artificial intelligence. Overall, the
research described in this dissertation demonstrates the consequential impact of human input on
algorithmic unfairness.

articles.title: When giving more makes you look worse: paradoxical inferences in a Bayesian model of social evaluation
articles.authors: Madeleine Horner; Victor Btesh; Adam Moore; Tadeg Quillien
articles.abstract:
People readily infer how much another agent cares about their
welfare, for example after observing this agent give them some of
what they have. However, these inferences become more difficult
when there is uncertainty over the resources someone has to share,
a common real-world scenario. We develop a Bayesian com-
putational model of how people infer the welfare trade-off ratio
(WTR) of another agent under resource uncertainty. This model
predicts that under uncertainty people should average over differ-
ent possible hypotheses about their partner’s resources. In a be-
havioral study (N = 129), we found that across donation amounts
participants’ WTR estimates closely tracked those made by our
Bayesian model. Notably, participants exhibited the following
paradoxical pattern predicted by our model: they sometimes saw
agents as less generous when they gave them more money, in
cases where a high donation revealed that the agent is rich and
is giving a comparatively small portion of their resources. These
findings demonstrate that people are able to make sophisticated
inferences consistent with rational Bayesian reasoning, updating
their beliefs about a partner’s resources and adjusting their WTR
estimates accordingly.

articles.title: Speech Cues Influence the Interpretation of Metaphoric Gestures
articles.authors: Laura Pissani; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Language and gesture are argued to form an integrated system where each modality contributes to the meaning of utterances. While gestures are known to influence language comprehension, less is known about how language affects the interpretation of gestures. We investigated whether the interpretation of metaphoric gestures is modulated by multimodal cues. Participants viewed hand gestures depicting the figurative interpretation of metaphors such as Men are oaks, where the actor flexed both arms to convey robustness while speaking. Gestures were presented with no additional cues, only lip movements, or audible speech. Participants then rated how well each gesture matched the gestured interpretation (e.g., robust), an alternative interpretation related to the metaphor but not to the gesture (e.g., tall), and an unrelated distractor (e.g., hikers). We found that ratings for alternative interpretations increased when gestures were paired with speech cues, compared to conditions without speech cues, suggesting that gesture comprehension is influenced by verbal content.

articles.title: What music do people use for mood regulation?
articles.authors: Catherine Tan; Peter M. C. Harrison
articles.abstract: This study seeks to understand what music is used for music mood regulation (MMR). We question how audio features can characterise the music used for different MMR strategies (Saarikallio and Erkkilä, 2007) and explore how participant background variables (musical sophistication, sensitivity to musical reward, empathy, and wellbeing) might predict the use of these strategies, and hence the kinds of music adopted for MMR. Participants (N = 229) completed an online survey that produced a dataset composed of 5,334 tracks for MMR, their Spotify audio feature measures, and scores for the participant background variables. Principal component analyses revealed that energetic and positive music was preferred for strategies that regulated positive moods or required arousal modulation, whereas instrumental music was preferred for strategies requiring cognitive responses and that of Strong Sensation. Regarding individual differences, results show that participants’ sensitivity to musical reward best correlated with their tendency to use all MMR strategies, and several other individual-difference measures were found to be associated with the use of specific MMR strategies to lesser degrees. We discuss the potential implications of our research for advancing theories on musical emotions and the development of wellbeing tools.

articles.title: Evidence for the absence of attentional biases towards stigma- and disease-related information in Atopic Dermatitis (preprint)
articles.authors: Henning Holle; Chloe North; Antoinette van Laarhoven; Shernaz Walton
articles.abstract: The hypervigilance hypothesis of psychosocial distress posits that individuals with visible skin conditions exhibit an attentional bias toward disease- or stigma-related information. While previous research has focused on psoriasis, the present study represents the first empirical test of the hypervigilance hypothesis in atopic dermatitis. Across a series of four pre-registered reaction time studies, we found no evidence that people with atopic dermatitis preferentially allocate their attention to words that are related to the sensory-affective or stigmatizing disease experience, relative to neutral control words. This was the case both during the early phase of stimulus disengagement, as well as during a later maintained attention phase dominated by controlled strategic processes. Overall, contrary to our expectations, results are incompatible with the hypervigilance hypothesis since the attention of people with atopic dermatitis was not biased towards the disease or stigma-related information.

articles.title: Cancer patients’ experiences of psychological function in multimodal prehabilitation: A systematic review and meta-ethnography
articles.authors: Aniqa Hussain; Thomas Albert Schroder; Mike Rennoldson; Michael Baliousis
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Multimodal prehabilitation is increasingly used to prepare patients for cancer treatment, yet the psychological functions of these programmes and how they are experienced by patients remain poorly understood. This review synthesises qualitative evidence on cancer patients’ experiences of multimodal prehabilitation programmes that included psychological elements.

Method: A systematic search of CINAHL, Medline, and PsycINFO was conducted to identify peer-reviewed qualitative studies exploring patient experiences of multimodal prehabilitation for cancer treatment. Eligible programmes explicitly aimed to address psychological outcomes or included elements with a stated psychological function. Study quality was appraised using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme. Findings were synthesised using meta-ethnography.

Results: Ten studies published between 2017 and 2023 were included. Three overarching constructs were identified: (1) the primacy of exercise within prehabilitation, with psychological processes often operating in synergy to support motivation, confidence, and adherence; (2) patient preferences for programme set-up, including personalisation, flexibility, timing, and mode of delivery; and (3) the wider context of patients’ lives and care pathways, including practical constraints, expectations about recovery and rehabilitation, and the involvement of significant others. Psychological benefits were often described as arising through exercise, social interaction, and supportive staff behaviours rather than through discrete psychological sessions. Overall study quality was acceptable, though reporting on staff training, intervention fidelity, and socioeconomic influences was limited.

Conclusions: Psychological processes are a prominent feature of patients’ experiences of multimodal prehabilitation and appear to shape engagement, acceptability, and perceived value across programme components. The findings support patient-centred, stratified approaches that integrate psychological function within routine prehabilitation delivery rather than positioning it as an optional add-on. Future research should prioritise co-design, include the perspectives of non-engagers, and articulate programme theories with embedded process evaluation to support effective and equitable implementation of prehabilitation in cancer care.

articles.title: Scoring Two-Part Multiple-Choice Items: An IRT Model Comparison with Implications for Validity Arguments and Process Inference
articles.authors: Juda Kaleta
articles.abstract: Two-part multiple-choice items elicit a content decision and a justifying reason, yet the choice of scoring rule that translates the paired responses into a latent-trait estimate is typically made on practical rather than psychometric grounds. We compare eight scoring models -- Part-1-only 2PL, conjunctive 2PL, partial credit, nominal response, mixed sequential, mixture IRT, attribute-group bifactor GPCM, and exploratory diagnostic classification -- fitted to two-part epistemic-knowledge data from N = 299 secondary-school students, mapped to Kane's argument-based validity framework, and stress-tested through a 100-replication simulation across five sample sizes (N = 50, 100, 200, 299, 500) under empirical generating parameters. No single model dominates. Information criteria are reported within comparable response-coding clusters, not across them. Within the polytomous cluster (M3, M7), the attribute-group bifactor generalized partial credit model (GPCM) achieves the lowest empirical AIC but converges in only 22-42% of simulation replications, indicating sample-specific rather than generalizable fit. Across binary recodings, the conjunctive two-parameter logistic (M2) yields lower BIC than the Part-1-only 2PL (M1) in 100% of N = 299 simulation replications, but this comparison is descriptive because the two models operate on different response codings; mixture IRT (M6), fitted on the Part-1 binary matrix, cannot reliably recover latent classes (Adjusted Rand Index near 0). Among non-mixture scoring models, person parameters diverge most consequentially between Part-1-only and conjunctive models (r = .82), demonstrating that scoring choices change score meaning, not merely precision. We propose a conditional framework that aligns each scoring model with an intended interpretation and the validity evidence it requires, treating the scoring rule as a substantive validity inference rather than a technical convenience.

articles.title: The Art of Making Problems Simple: A Theory of Intelligence
articles.authors: Angela J. Yu
articles.abstract: Human intelligence is remarkably flexible, capable of solving complex , novel problems and generalizing across domains with little training or data. We propose that intelligence depends on the ability to construct and revise representational normal forms: internal coordinate systems in which prediction, comparison, and inference become simple. This capacity rests on four components: (1) discovering lawful generators of variations in the world, (2) quotienting task-irrelevant distinctions, (3) constructing and selecting measurable structure for comparison, evaluation, and control, and (4) reorganizing representations so that task-relevant computations become simple and low-cost. The framework explains how these components arise during human development, from early object perception to abstract reasoning; why broad measures of fluid intelligence such as Raven’s matrices, analogical reasoning, and mental rotation generalize across domains; and how systematic individual cognitive differences can arise from trade-offs between representational precision and flexibility. Together, these results identify structural constraints on intelligence not captured by existing cognitive models or artificial intelligence systems.

articles.title: Prediction Errors Improve Declarative Learning but Not the Salience of Individual Words
articles.authors: Martin Benada; Filip Van Opstal
articles.abstract: Recent studies using the variable choice paradigm for word-association learning task suggest that reward prediction errors (RPEs) enhance one-shot declarative learning. However, these studies may confound true associative learning with an “RPE bias,” where high-RPE items become more salient or fluent and are therefore chosen during recognition. This study tested whether such a bias exists and whether RPEs genuinely strengthen word-pair learning. Across three sessions, participants (N = 48) completed a modified version of the variable choice paradigm in which English-Swahili word pairs were learned under either high-RPE or no-RPE conditions. Results provided moderate evidence against an RPE bias: high-RPE words were not more likely to be selected when incorrect. In contrast, correctness strongly predicted choice, and correct translations associated with high RPEs were moderately more likely to be chosen than correct translations without RPEs, indicating a true learning benefit. Confidence analyses mirrored this pattern, showing clear effects of correctness but inconclusive effects of RPE. Together, these findings demonstrate that RPEs improve associative memory without producing choice biases. Our results validate previous conclusions about RPE-driven declarative learning benefits.

articles.title: Cross-Modal Transfer of Statistical Learning: Evidence from auditory and visual sequence learning
articles.authors: Claudia Ruzza; Erin Isbilen; Richard Aslin
articles.abstract: Statistical learning (SL) enables the extraction of regularities across sensory modalities, yet it remains unclear whether learned representations are modality-specific or modality-general. Here, we investigated cross-modal generalization by exposing participants to spoken and written regularities using a sequential unimodal design with linguistic stimuli, manipulating the transfer direction (auditory-to-visual vs. visual-to-auditory), exposure order (visual-first vs. auditory-first), and task demands. Adults were tested using both a two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) task and a Statistically Induced Chunking Recall (SICR) task. Results showed robust cross-modal transfer, with no differences between modality-match and modality-mismatch trials and no directional asymmetries, indicating symmetric generalization across modalities. Exposure order affected 2AFC performance but not SICR, suggesting effects on explicit decision processes rather than implicit learning. Critically, recall revealed greater modality sensitivity than recognition, indicating that although learning transfers across modalities, modality-specific constraints emerge under more demanding retrieval conditions. These findings support a hybrid account in which SL operates over shared representations, while modality-specific constraints influence retrieval and behavioral expression under varying task demands.

articles.title: When and how music promotes fantasies: a survey on Maladaptive Daydreaming
articles.authors: Laura Ferreri; Lilya; Ilaria Bufalari; mario96; Marco Sperduti
articles.abstract: Daydreaming is a form of mind wandering involving structured, imaginative scenarios.  Maladaptive daydreaming (MD), or compulsive fantasy disorder, involves vivid fantasies that disrupt daily life. This study investigates the role of music in daydreaming, particularly its effects on MD. Using an online survey, we compared individuals with MD (MDers) to control participants to assess how music influences daydreaming. Our findings indicate that music (vs other media) was reported as the most effective tool for triggering and maintaining daydreaming, especially among MDers. Both groups indicated heightened emotional content in music-driven daydreams, but MDers experienced significantly longer, more vivid fantasies, while controls had more reality-anchored daydreams. Music-induced features, such as positive valence and pleasure, were found to play a main role in promoting daydreaming states. Furthermore, individual differences in musical absorption predicted MD severity. By bridging music cognition and clinical perspective, this study provides insights into the mechanisms of MD and the potential for music-based interventions to improve mental well-being.

articles.title: Does Choice Enhance Cognitive Control? Evidence for Indirect Effects via Choice History
articles.authors: Linda Carmen Bräutigam; Maximilian Mittenbühler; Martin V. Butz; Andrea Kiesel; Felix Cramer; David Dignath
articles.abstract:
We examined whether the opportunity to choose modulates context-specific cognitive control by contrasting two accounts: The Intention Hypothesis proposes that choice directly enhances control by enabling more precise predictions about upcoming demands. In contrast, the Inertia Hypothesis proposes an indirect effect: choice biases the temporal structure of context sequences, facilitating learning and retrieval of context–control associations.
Across four experiments, participants performed a flanker task with context-specific proportion congruency (CSPC) manipulations, in which one spatial context was associated with mostly congruent trials and another with mostly incongruent trials. We varied whether participants could choose the target location. Allowing choice increased CSPC effects relative to a No-Choice condition with random context order. Critically, this effect did not depend on the act of choosing itself. Comparable CSPC effects emerged in a No-Choice: yoked order condition, in which participants experienced the same context sequences without control over their occurrences. A computational model illustrates this by showing that an inertia-based mechanism, in which control accumulates over context-repetition, is sufficient to reproduce the results, whereas an intention-based mechanism does not capture the observed pattern. Consistent with the inertia account, individual differences in repetition bias of contexts predicted the magnitude of the CSPC effects. Together, these findings suggest that the effects of choice on cognitive control arise not from choice per se, but from the temporal structure that choice imposes on experience.

articles.title: Dynamics in subjective socioeconomic status matter over and above levels of socioeconomic status for self-regulation: Evidence across two longitudinal studies
articles.authors: Cameron Tan; Oh Yong Sheng Vincent; Ismaharif Ismail; Shuna Khoo Shi Ann; Han Sheng Ho; Lile Jia
articles.abstract: Do changes in socioeconomic status (SES) uniquely predict self-regulation beyond levels of SES? The present work examined whether changes in subjective and objective indices of SES predicted self-regulation relative to levels of SES. In Study 1 (Singapore, N=1737), latent change score modeling found that higher levels of subjective SES and increases in subjective SES predicted increased self-regulation over a year, while income and changes in income were unrelated to self-regulation. In Study 2 (U.S., N=7309), latent growth models across five waves (2015–2023) found that higher levels of subjective SES and increases in subjective SES predicted delayed gratification in 2024. Income levels were weakly related to delayed gratification, while income change was unrelated to delayed gratification. Together, these findings highlight the practical importance of supporting improvements in individuals’ perceived socioeconomic standing, suggesting that shifts in subjective status may represent a meaningful pathway for promoting self-regulation beyond changes in income alone.

articles.title: What Is There to Gain? A Systematic Review on Psychosocial Co-Benefits of Climate Policies
articles.authors: Ahlke Kip; Mira Tschorn; Susanne Vogel; Michèle Denise Birtel; Carla Hendricks; Anne Voigt; Silas Rooß; Julia Asbrand
articles.abstract: Background: Climate change poses major risks to psychosocial health, yet little is known about potential psychosocial co-benefits of climate policies. This systematic review examines (1) psychosocial co-benefits associated with climate policy measures and (2) moderating factors shaping these effects. Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, k = 21 studies were identified and analyzed using a PICOS framework and narrative synthesis. Results: Climate policies, particularly air pollution control, environmental taxation, and nature-based urban interventions, were associated with improvements in subjective well-being, mental health, and social cohesion. Effects were mediated by environmental improvements and physical health gains, and varied by socioeconomic status, age, and contexts such as schools, rural vs. urban. Access and actual use of interventions emerged as relevant but understudied mechanisms. Some studies indicated trade-offs, including unequal distribution of benefits and unintended social effects. Conclusions: Overall, climate policies can generate psychosocial co-benefits, but evidence remains heterogeneous and methodologically limited. Future research should strengthen causal inference, standardize outcome measures, and examine long-term and distributional effects.

articles.title: Perceptions of Teacher- Versus AI-Generated Feedback: Experimental Findings on the (Implicit) Bias of Teachers Against AI
articles.authors: Ute Mertens; Thorben Jansen; Marlene Steinbach; Johanna Fleckenstein; Jennifer Meyer
articles.abstract: Feedback is essential for enhancing student learning with complex tasks, but creating it is often time-consuming for teachers. Artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large language models, can support teachers in providing feedback. However, teachers’ use of AI depends on their perceptions of the AI-generated feedback. Drawing on the social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1979), it is hypothesised that teachers prefer feedback from fellow teachers (their ingroup) to AI-generated feedback (an outgroup); this leads to lower perception ratings of AI feedback compared to teacher feedback. This study experimentally examined whether the same feedback would be perceived negatively when labelled as “AI-generated” rather than when labelled as “teacher-generated” and whether providing teachers with additional information on AI’s functionality would influence this effect. A sample of 401 English-speaking teachers read three student essays, each with two AI-generated feedback messages, randomly labelled as AI-generated or teacher-generated. They indicated their perceptions of the feedback messages regarding credibility, usefulness, and fairness, as well as their feedback preference (teacher vs. AI). The results revealed that feedback perceptions were significantly less favourable for feedback labelled as AI-generated than for feedback labelled as teacher-generated, with the majority of teachers preferring teacher-labelled feedback to AI-labelled feedback (73% vs. 27%). These findings demonstrate that teachers systematically rate identical feedback as less credible, useful, and fair when it is labelled as AI-generated rather than teacher-generated, suggesting a negative bias that may have implications for the adoption of effective AI feedback in educational practice.

articles.title: Cross-structure variation in first and second language syntactic priming as evidence to distinguish models of priming.
articles.authors: Marion Coumel; Ema Ushioda; Katherine Messenger
articles.abstract: Speakers of all backgrounds tend to repeat the syntactic structures they hear, a phenomenon known as syntactic priming, and this effect influences not just their immediate language but also contributes to their long-term use and learning of structures. Yet the mechanisms underlying these processes remain the subject of debate. To tease apart the predictions of different classes of syntactic priming models, we took the unique approach of comparing immediate and long-term priming, with and without lexical overlap, in two populations of speakers and for two different structural alternations. We compared L2 and L1 French speakers’ (N=186) production of fronted/non-fronted temporal adverbial phrases and active/passive transitive structures. We replicated standard priming effects observing immediate and long-term priming, and lexical boost effects, across speakers and structures but we found that the magnitude of these effects differed between groups and structures. Abstract priming, both immediate and long-term, was greater for fronted than passive structures but did not differ between L2 and L1 groups, which is inconsistent with our predictions based on implicit learning models of syntactic priming. L2 speakers showed greater priming with lexical overlap and greater lexical boost effects than L1 speakers, which is consistent with developmental residual activation accounts that propose learners rely on lexicalised representations and short-term memory for primes. Our findings also suggest that accounts of syntactic priming should consider the role of production in supporting learning. This study shows the importance of comparing priming across speakers and for different structures for clarifying theoretical accounts of syntactic priming.

articles.title: FRIENDS - Friendship-based Regulation of Individual Emotions in Networks in Daily Situations - An EMA Study on Emotion Regulation Specialization and Social Outcomes in Adult Friendships
articles.authors: Paula Schott; Katharina Enser; Martin Weiß
articles.abstract: Interpersonal emotion regulation depends not only on specific strategies, but also on the choice of regulation partner. While previous research suggests that individuals select regulation partners based on their perceived competence in dealing with a specific emotion, we propose a strategy-based mechanism: individuals distribute their regulatory needs across different network members based on the specific strategies those friends provide. We examined how these processes unfold in everyday life using an Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) study. 106 participants tracked their social interactions over 14 days (total N = 2,796 interactions). In addition to partner selection and interaction outcomes, we calculated measures to quantify specialization within the network and assessed psychological well-being and loneliness. Results revealed a clear distinction between selection behaviors and interaction outcomes. Participants systemati-cally selected “go-to” friends for specific strategies (confirming behavioral preferences), but this selection pattern did not correspond with benefits regarding the interaction itself or psychological outcomes. We conclude that while individuals differentiate between partners for specific tasks, the psychological benefits of social interaction may result primarily from availability and mere presence rather than functional specialization, which aligns with the Social Baseline Theory proposed by Beckes and Coan (2011). The lack of association between network structure and subjective experience highlights the critical distinction between objective network structure and subjective perception of support.

articles.title: Historical differences in intrinsic work motivation in midlife in Germany
articles.authors: Georg Henning; Max Josef Rohrbacher; Andreas Stenling; Susanne Tafvelin; Martin Hyde
articles.abstract: Intrinsic work motivation is important for workers’ performance and well-being across all ages. Ongoing demographic change makes it particular important to study the work motivation of ageing workers, not the least because it may affect their later retirement decisions. The changing world of work, cohort differences in personality and health, as well as changing labor and pension policies may lead to historical differences in intrinsic work motivation, However, such effects have seldom been studied. In the present study, we examined historical differences in workers aged between 50 and 52 in either 2011 or 2022/23, based on two samples of the population-based German lidA survey. We found that those interviewed in 2022/23 showed slightly lower intrinsic work motivation (d = 0.11) than their earlier counterparts. Analyses also showed that opportunities for development and social support showed a stronger association with work motivation in the later-born sample, compared to the earlier cohort. Our results suggest that historical changes may affect work attitudes and that job demands and resources are possible targets to address workers’ work motivation.

articles.title: Data-driven profiles of psychopathology following moderate-severe traumatic brain injury
articles.authors: Jai Carmichael; Lucas Thoma; Gershon Spitz; Jennie Ponsford
articles.abstract:
Objective: To identify data-driven “prototypes” (distinct profiles) of psychopathology among adults with moderate-severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) using a transdiagnostic assessment battery, and to examine predictors of profile membership.

Setting: Community survey.

Participants: Participants (513 enrolled, 397 analyzed) were sampled from a larger cohort previously admitted consecutively for inpatient rehabilitation. They took part in the current study on average 12.63 years post-injury (range 0.40–35.42).

Design: Observational, cross-sectional.

Main Measures: A comprehensive battery of psychiatric questionnaires, including the Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms – Expanded Version (IDAS-II), Somatic Symptom Scale – 8 (SSS-8), Comprehensive Assessment of Traits Relevant to Personality Disorders (CAT-PD), and Externalizing Spectrum Inventory – Brief Form (ESI-BF). This battery was previously validated in the same sample to assess seven transdiagnostic dimensions of psychopathology: four internalizing (Detachment, Dysregulated Negative Emotionality, Somatic Symptoms, Compensatory and Phobic Reactions) and three externalizing (Self-Harm and Psychoticism, Rigid Constraint, Harmful Substance Use).

Results: Latent profile analysis identified five profiles: Average Internalizing (29% of participants most resembled this profile), Lower Psychopathology (25%), Elevated Internalizing (21%), Substance Use (16%), and Elevated Psychopathology (10%). In multivariable analyses, younger age, greater self-reported functional disability, lower life satisfaction, and mental health treatment were associated with more severe psychopathology profiles. Male sex and unemployment were associated with the Substance Use profile. Those who were fewer years post-injury and in a relationship were more likely to show a Lower Psychopathology profile relative to Average Internalizing. Education, Glasgow Coma Scale scores, and comorbid orthopedic injuries showed no significant associations with psychopathology profiles.

Conclusions: A data-driven categorical approach identified psychopathology profiles following moderate-severe TBI that differed primarily in overall severity. Profiles reflecting greater psychopathology were associated with poorer functioning and subjective well-being, providing some validation. However, clinically relevant variation in post-TBI psychopathology may not readily reduce to a small number of qualitatively discrete categories.

articles.title: Factors Associated with Mental Health Care Utilization Among Individuals with Predominately Compensable Moderate-Severe Traumatic Brain Injury
articles.authors: Jai Carmichael; Gershon Spitz; Kate Rachel Gould; Jennie Ponsford
articles.abstract:
Objective: To document and identify determinants of mental health care utilization in individuals with predominately compensable moderate-severe traumatic brain injury (TBI).

Design: Cross-sectional survey.

Setting: Australian community.

Participants: Adults with moderate-severe TBI (N=386; mean time since injury=12.88 years, range=0.40–35.02), sampled from a larger longitudinal cohort (N=4502) consecutively admitted for inpatient rehabilitation in the context of a no-fault accident compensation system, with the majority (88.83%) having access to compensable funding for ongoing care.

Interventions: Not applicable.

Main Outcome Measure(s): Self-reported psychiatric medication use, emotional counseling, and past-year psychiatric hospitalization.

Results: Mental health care utilization was reported by 34% of participants, including 26% receiving emotional counseling, 21% using psychiatric medication, and 3% hospitalized for psychiatric reasons in the past year, compared with 16% pre-injury—a significant increase (P<.001). Multivariable logistic regression identified factors associated with mental health care utilization, adjusting for severity of depression and anxiety symptoms. Independent of higher symptom severity (P=.004), greater mental health care utilization was associated with younger age (P=.024), pre-injury mental health treatment (P<.001), being out of the labor force (P=.022), and fewer years post-injury (P=.011). Furthermore, greater self-reported functional disability predicted medication use (P=.046), while higher Glasgow Coma Scale score (P=.046) and conversely longer duration of post-traumatic amnesia (P=.032) predicted emotional counseling.

Conclusions: Age, pre-injury treatment history, employment status, time since injury, and ongoing disability may be associated with mental health care utilization beyond what can be accounted for by differences in symptom severity alone. These findings suggest that mental health care utilization following moderate-severe TBI does not consistently correspond to the level of emotional distress or clinical need, even within a sample with predominantly compensable injuries. Further research is needed to better understand these factors to ensure that individuals experiencing mental health difficulties are able to engage in appropriate treatment.

articles.title: Human judgments and machine learning reveal the perceptual diversity and contextual malleability of real-life emotional vocalizations
articles.authors: Doron Atias; Tal Bamberger; Omri Peleg; Hillel Aviezer
articles.abstract: Emotional vocalizations are central to human social life yet researchers debate their nature. Classic theories posit that human vocalizations convey accurate and reliable information about emotion. However, past research relied mostly on posed and stereotypical expressions which diverge from naturalistic signals. Across two preregistered studies (N=842), we examined the diagnosticity and contextual malleability of real-life laughter and crying vocalizations. Using human judgments and machine learning, we demonstrate that real-life laughter and crying are perceptually diverse, with ~40% of the analyzed vocalizations conveying ambiguous and atypical affective messages. This diversity was also reflected by the distinct acoustic profiles of typical versus atypical vocalizations. Finally, we show that co-occurring visual context has a profound impact on the perception of real-life vocalizations, especially when vocal cues are ambiguous. Together, these findings demonstrate that real-life vocalizations convey both typical and atypical affective cues and highlight the striking role of contextual information and multimodal integration in facilitating emotion understanding in everyday life.

articles.title: Limits of the illusory truth effect for social-political opinions: Evidence from two experiments and a mini meta-analysis
articles.authors: Paul Riesthuis; Josh Aaron Woods
articles.abstract: Repeated exposure to information often increases its perceived truthfulness, a phenomenon known as the illusory truth effect. Although this effect is well established for factual statements, it remains unclear whether repetition similarly influences truth judgments for evaluative opinion statements. The present research tested whether repetition affects subjective truth judgments for social political opinion statements and whether this effect differs depending on participants’ level of agreement with the statements. Across two preregistered experiments (N = 457), participants were exposed to novel and repeated opinion statements and provided truth and agreement ratings either during encoding (Experiment 2) or at separate stages (Experiment 1). We evaluated repetition effects both overall and separately for statements participants agreed with, disagreed with, or rated as neutral. Across both experiments, repetition did not reliably increase truth judgments for social political opinion statements. Equivalence tests indicated that any repetition effects were smaller than a predefined smallest effect size of interest. This pattern was consistent across agreement categories, with no evidence of meaningful repetition effects for statements participants agreed with, disagreed with, or evaluated as neutral. A mini meta-analysis combining the present findings with prior work further supported the conclusion that repetition effects for opinion statements are negligible within the tested bounds. These findings suggest that repetition-based increases in perceived truth may not readily generalize to social political opinions. More broadly, the results constrain the conditions under which fluency-based processes influence truth judgments, indicating limited transfer to domains characterized by subjective or evaluative content.

articles.title: Cryptic Re-perception: A Cognitive Framework for Creative-Critical Thinking
articles.authors: Andrew Veda; Luke Gerard Christie
articles.abstract:
Abstract:
Contemporary models of thinking frequently distinguish between creativity, defined as the generation of novel ideas, and critical thinking, defined as the evaluation of those ideas. While this distinction has been influential across cognitive science and education, it leaves underexamined a fundamental component of reasoning: the process by which problems themselves are interpreted and represented. This paper introduces cryptic re-perception as a distinct cognitive operation grounded explicitly in the cognitive and neural mechanisms of cryptic crossword solving, a domain that requires solvers to deliberately inhibit misleading surface readings and construct latent, non-obvious interpretations from the same linguistic material. Drawing on Ohlsson’s Representational Change Theory, predictive processing accounts of cognition, dual-process theories, research on insight problem solving, and the emerging AI literature on cryptic crossword disambiguation, the paper argues that many failures in reasoning arise not from deficits in idea generation or evaluation, but from fixation on initial interpretations shaped by prior experience. Critically, the paper addresses a gap in prior frameworks: the expansion grid proposed to operationalise structured reinterpretation is distinguished from existing heuristic tools such as SCAMPER and de Bono’s Thinking Hats by its grounding in semantic network dynamics and its derivation from attested neural mechanisms of inhibitory control and associative expansion. The paper further integrates this model with contemporary neuroscience and identifies three falsifiable predictions that distinguish cryptic re-perception from these prior frameworks and discusses an empirical research programme needed to test them. Real-world implications are examined across domains including education, decision-making, and human–artificial intelligence interaction, where the consistent failure of large language models on cryptic crossword benchmarks provides a concrete, measurable index of the reinterpretation gap the framework addresses.
Keywords: cryptic re-perception, creative-critical thinking, representational change, predictive processing, insight, inhibitory control, large language models, cryptic crosswords

articles.title: Conceptual diversity of linguistic structure: a mixed-methods corpus study
articles.authors: Wiktor Rorot; Joanna Raczaszek-Leonardi
articles.abstract: The goal of this paper is to uncover how conceptualizations of linguistic structure differ between the practice of research on language emergence and standard, formalized approaches of philosophy of language and theoretical linguistics. We analyze a corpus of 5000 scientific articles within the field of language emergence. Adopting a mixed-methods approach, we combine computational analysis—semantic search, topic modelling, and collocation analysis—with qualitative close reading. Results indicate that researchers in language emergence view linguistic structure as a complex phenomenon, which cuts across some existing theoretical distinctions, most importantly between “compositional” and “contextual” accounts. These results indicate the need for a new view of the systematicity of linguistic structure.

articles.title: Increasing the effectiveness of charitable giving with AI-generated persuasion
articles.authors: Joshua Paul White; Carter Allen; Lucius Caviola; Thomas Costello; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: Charitable donations frequently fail to maximize cost-effectiveness (the amount of good a donation does per dollar). This failure is often attributed to charitable motivations being affective and thus insensitive to evidence-based arguments. We challenge this perspective, hypothesizing that evidence can substantially increase effective giving—if that evidence is sufficiently compelling. We test this prediction in a pre-registered experiment (N = 1,949 Americans) by leveraging the ability of artificial intelligence large language models (LLMs) to generate persuasive content. Participants allocated $1 between their favorite charity and a highly effective charity (the Against Malaria Foundation), before and after an LLM dialogue or a static LLM message advocating for the effective charity, or a control conversation. The LLM dialogue and static LLM message both significantly increased effective donations (45.9% and 28.7%, respectively) in comparison to control, while the LLM dialogue also shifted moral attitudes. Effective giving can be meaningfully increased through evidence-based persuasion with LLMs.

articles.title: Priming Cognitive Flexibility in Reappraisal
articles.authors: Jeremiah Haravon Collins; Conrado Eiroa-Solans
articles.abstract: Cognitive reappraisal has long been shown to support adaptive emotion regulation, yet direct evidence about how to increase its effectiveness remains limited. We propose that increasing cognitive flexibility prior to the emotional stimulus may enhance reappraisal success. In this study, we tested whether priming participants with bistable images would increase the effectiveness of two reappraisal tactics—reconstrual (changing a situation’s meaning) and repurposing (changing the goals or values through which it is evaluated). In a 2 (prime: bistable vs. control) × 2 (reappraisal: reconstrual vs. repurposing) × 5 (repetition) within-subjects experiment, 193 undergraduates (M_age = 20.79; 75% female) viewed a prime, watched a negative video, rated their affect, generated a written reappraisal using the assigned tactic, and then re-rated their affect on each trial. Change in negative affect was analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts for participants, primes, and videos, and maximal random slopes for fixed effects. Bistable images elicited more perceptual shifts than control images but did not improve reappraisal effectiveness overall or differentially benefit reconstrual over repurposing. Across priming conditions, however, reconstrual produced a greater reduction in negative affect than repurposing. These findings suggest that stimulus-irrelevant flexibility cues do not readily transfer to affective flexibility in emotion regulation and highlight a modest advantage of reconstrual over repurposing in reducing negative affect within the present context. Future work should examine whether flexibility cues that are semantically related to the emotional stimulus—or embedded within it—more readily transfer to affective flexibility during reappraisal, and clarify the conditions under which reconstrual yields greater benefits than repurposing.

articles.title: Identifying individual difference correlations when the data are noisy: A case study of proactive and reactive control
articles.authors: Jeffrey N. Rouder; Shanglin Yang; Todd Samuel Braver
articles.abstract: The Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework postulates two distinct and semi-independent modes of cognitive control.  Proactive control is hypothesized to be anticipatory, engaged prior to stimulus onset, whereas reactive control is triggered by stimulus or response features.  In the current study, we test a key prediction of this framework: that individual differences in proactive control and reactive control should be weakly correlated, if at all.  There is a key challenge faced by correlational analyses: measurement error will systematically lower correlation values; so much so that observing a small (or non-existent) correlation is difficult to interpret.  To address this challenge, we develop a hierarchical model that appropriately estimates measurement error and individual covariation simultaneously.
